title: Lesson 214
transcript: We're still in domain number one, which as you know is all about security management, how we manage the security requirements of the organization and how we should always be aligned and contributing towards goals and objectives. In this module we're going to talk about how security controls should apply to people and what this is referred to as in the exam outline from ISE squared is personnel security policy. So these are policies and controls that apply to people. And if you think about where we have to apply security controls to people, it's at different times or parts of your employment, for example. From an organizational perspective, when you hire people or before you hire people, you need to do some things related to security like background checks, for example, or verify educational records. But then once that person has been employed and hired, for example, there are security controls that need to be applied to that person as they're doing their job throughout employment. And then of course at some point in the future, When the employment agreement is terminated for whatever reason, whether the person leaves on their own behalf or retires or goes to work for someone else, There needs to be certain things that need to get done from a security perspective, like removing user access and enforcing non-disclosure agreements, for example. And so those are the types of security controls that we need to think about in this section. There's also one more part of this that applies to people. and that is organizations today rely on a lot of third-party services like for example cloud providers or you could have services that are being provided by third parties so you may have to apply security policies to those third parties as well and what we want to remember about that is that the only way that you can achieve security and get them to conform for example to your security policies is through contracts agreements non-disclosure agreements and service level agreements and so on so that's really a summary of this entire module here within the security management domain. So if you start at candidate screening and at the time that you hire people, for example, One thing that you might want to do is give them job descriptions that include specific security capabilities or responsibilities that they need to take care of. If someone has something in their job description that is specifically related to security, they're going to take that seriously because they know that they're being measured on something that is in their job description. So if we've got the luxury of a job description, usually is a good thing from a security perspective. And before you hire people, obviously see here how you need to check things like employment history and their educational background. Verify that they have the skills that they say they have so that they can perform the job. Maybe in some cases you want to be able to do criminal checks and reference checks as well. Now these are all examples of security controls that obviously get done before you hire or at the time of hiring. And as we've said all along, what we need to remember about any type of security control is that they should always be cost effective. And so whether you do all of these or not is typically based on what that person is going to be doing for the organization. and the value that it represents. You always want security controls to be cost-effective which at a minimum means that the cost of the control itself is less than The value that you're trying to protect. That is always a really important goal to achieve in security. If a security control is not cost effective, we don't implement it. It's as simple as that because in that case you are not contributing to adding value to the organization and in fact you are taking away from it. Now during your employment with an organization, this slide shows some of the types of security controls that, again, organizations may implement that relate to people as part of their employment. So we've got acceptable use policies, non-disclosure agreements for certain people that are working with very sensitive information. You may impose some non-compete agreements on people, for example. And something that should apply to everyone at all levels of the organization is a code of ethics or a code of conduct that typically companies will impose on everyone. and so on. So these are examples of controls that are applied during your employment and in fact this slide continues on with that theme. Other examples like for example job rotation and some companies call this rotation of duties but it simply means that you take someone out of a job and you move someone else into it. And from a security perspective, that can be very effective. There are some industries, for example, like the financial industries banks may implement something like this and if you think about what kind of a control this provides if you move someone out of a job and you place someone into it It can be a preventive type of a control because if I know that someone is moving into my job, it may prevent me from doing something that is unethical or against policy, for example. And if someone is moving into my job, it can also be a detective type of a control because if someone moves into my job, they would be able to detect that I am doing something that is unethical or against policy or even illegal. So it can also be a detective type of a control. And finally, job rotation can also be a corrective type of a control. Because if somebody moves into my job and notices that I was doing something against policy, obviously they're going to fix it. So something like job rotation is one of those controls that we said is very effective. And we've said all along in this course, and you should always remember, that a good control or a good complete control is one that can prevent, detect, and correct. And something like job rotation is a perfect example of that. Other examples of controls that could be applied during your employment as a person that works within the organization, separation of duties. And that's a favorite one for a lot of industries and a lot of organizations. Some companies also call it segregation of duties. And it simply means that we separate a task so that it cannot be completed by one individual alone. It needs to involve more than one individual. and from a security perspective that's obviously very good because it can prevent fraud and it forces collusion doesn't it because if you want to do something that is illegal or against policy you're going to have to involve someone else and that in itself can be preventive or a really good deterrent but it can also be more detective and corrective as well so these are examples of really good controls and we've already talked about how how we implement access controls in the organization should always focus on need to know least privilege concepts And I think we know exactly what that means. We only give you what you absolutely need to know to perform your job function. And we only give you the least privilege that you require with that. So those are good controls that should always be applied. And finally, mandatory vacations. Again, not a control that you would see typically implemented in all organizations, but forcing people to take vacations at certain times of the year. would force that person to be replaced for that time that they're away on vacation. So it can be a control just like job rotation and therefore provide preventive, detective and corrective capabilities. are again good examples of controls. Now one thing that we need to remember however is that as we've said multiple times, controls always need to be cost effective. So whether you implement those controls throughout the entire organization, or whether you only do it where the value is such that it warrants those controls, is a decision that obviously the organization needs to make. But here's a perfect example. Separation of duties should not be implemented in all areas of the organization. You would only implement that where it makes sense from a cost effectiveness perspective. So any of these controls, as well as any other that we talk about in this class, should always be cost effective, meaning it costs less than the value that you're trying to protect. It's always value that drives security in any organization, not the risk, because the risk is something that can ultimately impact value. So value is what drives security. The risk is something that can impact that value, which is important to understand as well. But it's value that drives security. And so where the value is such, that's where we implement any type of control. Now these ones is just a summary here that you see on this slide of some of the controls. Anytime you see a slide in this presentation, it's not meant to be an all-inclusive complete list. we're simply giving you examples but maybe meaningful examples that you might see somewhere that is meaningful to you for example but onboarding when you're bringing people into the organization you know some of the controls that you would apply related to security are the ones that you see here and at termination as well termination doesn't just mean someone's gotten fired and therefore security controls need to be applied. Termination also means the job has been terminated for whatever reason, maybe they're retiring or or going somewhere else to work, but the employment contract has been terminated which forces security to activate and do certain things related to, you know, like removing user access and enforcing non-disclosure agreements. But again, only with those people where the value is such that we need to enforce those security controls. Now, we did mention at the beginning of this that personnel security policies are controls that apply to people. But the way that the organizations operate today, they rely heavily on third party services. And so a lot of security controls need to be applied to vendors and contractors and consultants, for example, that the organization has come in and work with certain things that obviously represent value. And so the only way that you can enforce security controls to third parties that don't really belong to the organization is through contracts and agreements and service level agreements that security needs to be involved in and enforce as well. But these, as we'll see later, is also something that requires us to really understand the requirements before we can write those into the contracts and then we can enforce. those contracts and that's why security needs to be involved really in the contract negotiation at the beginning of the relationship with third party vendors and services and so on. so that we can write in the requirements and then enforce those using security controls such as contracts and agreements. Now one of the things that we have to highlight here and reinforce is that people within the organization may be working with things related to privacy like personal data for example or PII which as you know is in reference to personally identifiable information And because these are driven by very strict laws and regulations that govern privacy, it's very important to ensure that the people that are working with anything that relates to privacy are aware of what they might do in order to make the organization, for example, not conform to privacy laws. So this is where security comes in. We need to provide education, awareness, training, but also make sure that the people that are working with any personal data have the skills and the right architectures to be able to enforce privacy laws and ultimately the compliance requirements that organizations would have to meet on a regular basis. And that's why all organizations should have a privacy policy that can dictate very clearly what the expectations not only from a compliance perspective is, but also the accountabilities and responsibilities that each one of those employees would have related to privacy and so on. And this is where security comes in. we need to make sure that we communicate those policies and allow individuals, whether they're accountable or responsible, to be able to meet the requirements of those policies so that ultimately the organization meets its compliance requirements related to privacy legislation.
